title: White House Ethics Deal on CLARITY Act Moves Senate Vote Odds to 42-43%
dek: The White House agreed on ethics-provision language for the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act and circulated it to Senate Republicans on July 20. The language is designed to bar senior government officials from holding or profiting from digital assets they regulate. Passage odds on prediction markets rose to the low-to-mid 40% range following the report.
author: Crypto Cronkite
key_fact: White House ethics language circulated to Senate GOP narrows the obstruction Democrats had named as a condition for their support, but the 60-vote threshold and compressed August recess timeline mean passage remains uncertain.
bottom_line: The ethics text is circulating but not yet public, and Democratic senators have not yet committed to supporting it. Watch for any Democratic statement of support in the next week and calendar signals from Senate leadership about floor scheduling before August recess. If Democrats reject the compromise or no floor vote is scheduled before early August, the 40-plus odds would likely deflate.
body: The White House reached agreement on ethics-provision language for the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act and circulated the agreed text to Senate Republicans on July 20, according to The Defiant. The move marks the first reported breakthrough in months on the stalled cryptocurrency-regulatory bill and lifted passage odds on Polymarket into the low-to-mid 40% range, up from the high-80% range earlier in the year before the ethics deadlock set in.

The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act (H.R. 3633) would establish a federal market-structure framework for digital assets, splitting oversight between the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission, and codify rules that currently rest on agency guidance. The bill passed the House in July 2025 and cleared the Senate Banking Committee in May 2026 but has stalled on the Senate floor over a single issue: ethics rules for senior government officials.

Democratic senators named the ethics provision as a condition of their support. The proposed language is designed to bar senior government officials from holding or profiting from digital assets they are responsible for regulating. The stated rationale reflects a factual premise: President Trump's 2025 financial disclosure, released by the Office of Government Ethics on July 1, showed roughly $1.4 billion in crypto-related income, including royalties from the $TRUMP meme coin and proceeds tied to World Liberty Financial. Democrats argued that building a federal framework for digital assets from which the sitting president had drawn his largest single source of income required rules governing official conflicts.

White House crypto adviser Patrick Witt stated that any ethics language must apply uniformly to all officials rather than single out the president or his family. An earlier compromise that would have let state attorneys general enforce ethics violations was rejected by Democrats as insufficient, according to The Defiant's reporting.

The specific text of the ethics agreement has not been made public. A Senate Banking Committee ethics amendment offered by Sen. Chris Van Hollen was defeated on a party-line vote at the May markup. Reported confirmation came on July 21 when The Defiant cited multiple industry sources on remarks by reporter Eleanor Terrett about the White House sending the ethics language to certain Senate Republicans on July 20.

Market reaction was modest. Bitcoin traded around $66,700, up roughly 2 percent over 24 hours, with roughly $31 billion in daily volume. Ether was near $1,930, up about 1.8 percent. XRP rose close to 4 percent to $1.15. Solana added under 1 percent at roughly $78.

An ethics agreement is necessary to unlock floor scheduling but not sufficient to pass the bill. The 60-vote threshold in the Senate means several Democrats must cross over for passage. The Senate is expected to begin its August recess in early August, leaving a narrow window for a floor vote. The deal language circulating among Republicans will need to satisfy Democratic holdouts before any vote is scheduled.
